one.en: PROPERTY – Interim proceedings – Where an application for payment of children’s private school fees – where prior application for payment dismissed – …

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Interim proceedings – Where an application for payment of children’s private school fees – where prior application for payment dismissed – where one party opposes children’s attendance at private schools – whether accrued school fees constitute joint interim liability – no order for payment. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where application to vary date fixed for sale of real property – absence of material change in circumstances – discretion not enlivened. FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Jointly owned business – agreement in principle to sell – orders regulating conduct of sale – restraint on unilateral communications. FAMILY LAW – VALUATION – Whether updated valuation required where business to be sold – limited forensic utility – updated valuation ordered for remaining business interests only.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

INTRODUCTION

This matter is listed for a final hearing in relation to property to commence on 10 August 2026.
The parties resolved their parenting dispute, entering into final consent orders on 27 January 2026.
The parties also agreed that the properties at F Street, Suburb G and H Street, Suburb J would be sold forthwith in accordance with the orders made on 30 October 2025. I am not of the view that it is necessary at this stage to provide the wife with sole conduct of the sale in the event the husband fails to sign documents required to proceed with the sale.
The interim matters requiring determination are:
(a) whether any order should be made for the payment of accrued private school fees;

(b) whether the date for the sale of the property at D Street, Suburb E (Suburb E property) should be varied;

(c) what order should be made regulating the conduct of the sale of K Pty Ltd; and

(d) whether an updated valuation of K Pty Ltd should be obtained.

By consent the subpoena objections listed on 23 February 2026 were adjourned to a later date.

THE EVIDENCE

The husband relied on:
(a) the Application in a Proceeding filed 2 December 2025;

(b) his Financial Statement filed 16 February 2026;

(c) his affidavit filed 16 February 2026; and

(d) the case outline filed 20 February 2026.

The wife relied on:
(a) the Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 17 December 2025;

(b) her Enforcement Application filed 3 February 2025; and

(c) her affidavit filed on 13 February 2026.

I also had the benefit of written submissions from counsel for the wife.

MATTERS TO BE DETERMINED

The children’s school fees

The child X is enrolled at L School, and Y at M School.
The children’s enrolment at those schools – and the payment of those fees – are in dispute.
In May 2024 interim orders were made by consent for the children’s school fees to be paid from the parties’ offset account #98. Those funds have now been exhausted.
In October 2025 the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking inter alia that the children’s tuition fees be paid from a different offset account.
At that time, the wife opposed further joint funds being used to meet the children’s school fees, asserting she does not have the financial capacity to contribute to the fees. It is common ground that neither parent has the financial capacity to meet the children’s fees from their income. It is also common ground there is a child support assessment in place. The husband is in arrears of $5,754.34.
It is the wife’s case that the children should attend a government school in circumstances where neither parent is able to meet the costs from their respective incomes. Her counsel acknowledged the orders sought in her client’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding that the children be enrolled at N School is a parenting order. Parenting proceedings were resolved by consent on 27 January 2026. Counsel appropriately conceded this is not properly a matter that can be pressed at this time, and if the wife wishes to seek orders regarding the children’s schooling, she will need to amend her Initiating Application to do so.
However, at this interim hearing, the wife continues to oppose the payment of the school fees that have now accrued from capital. She seeks to preserve the tangible asset pool to ensure she is able to appropriately house herself and the children following final property orders being made.
The husband’s application for the payment of school fees was dismissed on 30 October 2025.
The husband filed a further Application in a Proceeding on 2 December 2025 again seeking – among other things – the payment of the children’s school fees from a joint account. In particular, he sought “all outstanding and future compulsory school fees and charges” for the children be paid as and when they fall due.
Counsel for the husband properly conceded that in the absence of any child support departure application by the husband no source of power has been identified to order the payment going forward for school fees.
However, counsel for the husband asserted that the school fees that have accrued are a joint liability of the parties, and accordingly, the court could make an order for their payment in the exercise of its power under s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). As at 16 February 2026 the fees owing to L School were $6,114.05, and the fees owing to M School were $21,952.77.
According to the statement from L School dated 16 February 2026, the entirety of currently outstanding fees were billed on 2 February 2026.
In October 2025, M School rendered accounts for 2026 Term 1 tuition fees ($10,315), music lessons ($600.38) and a building fund donation ($90).
Those amounts were due for payment on 1 November 2025 – being one day after I dismissed the husband’s original application for the payment of the fees. They were incurred for the 2026 school year and accrued in the face of the wife’s opposition to the children’s attendance at private schools.
In his affidavit filed 16 February 2026 the husband asserted the total amount owing to M School was $21,952.77 which he said was “due for payment on 15 February 2026”.
The wife opposes the payment of any fees from joint accounts. She asserted that had the children been withdrawn from their schools in 2025 following the dismissal of the husband’s application for the payment of the fees – no additional fees for the 2026 school year would have accrued.
I am not satisfied that it is appropriate, in circumstances where the Court has previously declined to make such an order and where one party continues to oppose the children’s attendance at private schools, to characterise the accrued fees as a joint interim liability merely because they have now been invoiced.
Accordingly, I will not make the order for the payment of the school fees from joint monies as an interim property determination. When the matter proceeds by way of final hearing I anticipate I will be requested to determine at that time how the payment of outstanding fees will be made as part of the final property division. If an application is made by the husband for the payment of future school fees, that will need to be properly articulated as a child support order. In the exercise of the Court’s interim property jurisdiction, I am not persuaded that such an order is just or appropriate at this stage.

Timing of the sale of Suburb E property

Orders were made on 30 October 2025 for the sale of several properties. Those orders included that the Suburb E property was to be placed on the market for sale no later than 15 January 2026.
It is the wife’s application to postpone the sale of the Suburb E property for six months. That is sought in her Response to an Application in a Proceedings filed on 17 December 2025.
The wife and children currently reside in the former matrimonial home in Suburb O. That property was sold in early 2026, with an extended settlement to take place in July 2026. This information was provided in a joint letter from the parties’ practitioners after the interim hearing had concluded. The wife asserted the long settlement was at the request of the purchaser. The husband advised he consented to that lengthy settlement to enable the wife to secure alternate accommodation and to minimise the disruption to the children’s living and schooling arrangements.
The wife does not intend to retain the Suburb E property as part of any final property settlement. However, she says she intends to move with the children into the Suburb E property for a period, so that the children can be enrolled at N School. It is her position that the children’s current school enrolments cannot be sustained. To enable that enrolment, the children must reside in the Suburb E property (or other property in the catchment zone). The husband does not agree that the children should be enrolled at N School.
As already observed, final parenting orders were made on 27 January 2026. Those orders included that the parents were to make joint decisions regarding the children’s education. There is no parenting application on foot before me and accordingly I have no power to make orders regarding the children’s school enrolment.
I am not satisfied that there has been any material change in circumstances since the orders made on 30 October 2025 that would justify further delaying the sale. I accept the trial was at that time listed to commence on 13 February 2026 and has now been adjourned to August 2026. However, the wife would have been aware she would likely require alternative accommodation for herself and the children pending final orders even had the trial commenced in February 2026. The wife and children are able to remain in the Suburb O property until July 2026, and no evidence establishes that the sale of the Suburb E property as ordered would result in housing instability or other hardship warranting intervention. There are also no circumstances that have arisen that make the sale impracticable.

Sale of K Pty Ltd

The orders made on 19 December 2025 noted that the parties agreed in principle to the sale of K Pty Ltd. However, they have been unable to agree as to orders for the conduct of that sale.
The wife proposes the parties have joint conduct of the sale and that both parties are included in all communications about the sale. She says that is necessary as she has significant concerns the husband may act unilaterally. She asserts the husband has not made proper disclosure and has maintained sole control over the business. She has no confidence that he will keep her properly informed regarding the sale, and has concerns he may dispose of the business on inappropriate terms.
I accept these requirements may be administratively burdensome. However, given the issues that have arisen regarding disclosure, I am satisfied that the orders proposed by the wife are appropriate to ensure that each party is properly kept informed and neither can act unilaterally in relation to the sale.

Valuation of K Pty Ltd

Trial directions made on 19 December 2025 provided that the parties were to instruct the Single Expert to prepare an updated valuation report of the parties’ business, referred to in the valuation report dated 18 March 2025 as “the Group”.
It is common ground the wife has not signed the letter of instruction to the valuer prepared by the husband. That is because the letter instructs the valuations of only B Pty Ltd and C Pty Ltd be updated. The wife asserts that in accordance with the orders made on 19 December 2025 the entire group – inclusive of K Pty Ltd is to be valued.
The husband asserts that there is no utility in updating the value of K Pty Ltd in circumstances where it is agreed the entity is to be sold. He says the relevant value will be the sale value. That is different to his position at the time he filed his Application in a Proceeding on 2 December 2025. In the affidavit supporting that application the husband deposed that the valuation already prepared might not be adequate for the purposes of these proceedings, and that an updated valuation ought to be prepared once up to date financial statements were completed. The husband further deposed that he had prepared the updated financial reports for each of the entities and said “given the concerns previously raised about the adequacy of the earlier report and the time that had passed, I considered it reasonable and appropriate to obtain a further valuation on a joint basis”. He said that an updated independent valuation of each of the entities was “necessary to enable the court to make a just and equitable property settlement”.
Notwithstanding the entity is to be sold, the wife asserts it should be included in the updated valuation. She says at the time the order was made on 19 December 2025 for the entity to be included in the updated valuation, the sale of the business was foreshadowed as set out in the notations to those orders. It is her case that the business may not have been sold prior to the final hearing, and a valuation will enable the Court to ascertain the parties’ assets and liabilities at trial. The wife says an updated valuation will also assist in understanding the reasons for any potential downturn in the value of the business. She is suspicious of the reduction in the business income and the husband’s remuneration and is concerned the husband may be diverting business income to other entities. It is also her case that there has been a significant lack of transparency regarding K Pty Ltd and its operations post separation, which have been solely controlled by the husband. She says there have been significant issues with disclosure.
I am not satisfied there is utility in an updated valuation of K Pty Ltd given there is an agreement the entity be sold. In those circumstances, the marginal forensic utility of an interim valuation does not justify the cost involved.
If the wife’s case continues to be that the husband has reduced the value of K Pty Ltd by funnelling income and clients into other entities she can adduce evidence in support of that claim at the final hearing and cross examine the husband as to his management of the business. It would not be the role of the Single Expert to speculate on these issues or undertake a tracing exercise.
It may be that K Pty Ltd is not sold prior to the final hearing. However, that will not delay the trial, as orders are frequently made prior to the sale of assets.
Accordingly, the updated valuation will be in relation to B Pty Ltd and C Pty Ltd only.
For these reasons, I make the orders as are set out.
